vulnerability.title: Set Account Expiration Following Inactivity
vulnerability.description: To specify the number of days after a password expires (which signifies inactivity) until an account is permanently disabled, add or correct the following line in /etc/default/useradd: INACTIVE= If a password is currently on the verge of expiration, then day(s) remain(s) until the account is automatically disabled. However, if the password will not expire for another 60 days, then 60 days plus day(s) could elapse until the account would be automatically disabled. See the useradd man page for more information.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q shadow-utils; }; then var_account_disable_post_pw_expiration='' # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^INACTIVE") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s=%s" "$stripped_key" "$var_account_disable_post_pw_expiration" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C grep -q -m 1 -i -e "^INACTIVE\\>" "/etc/default/useradd"; then escaped_formatted_output=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/

triage_decision.reason: Disabling accounts after password expiration reduces the attack surface by ensuring inactive accounts cannot be used.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Accounts remain enabled after password expiration; Inactive accounts may be exploited

execution_details.stdout:
# useradd defaults file
GROUP=100
HOME=/home
INACTIVE=-1
EXPIRE=
SHELL=/bin/bash
SKEL=/etc/skel
CREATE_MAIL_SPOOL=yes

# useradd defaults file
GROUP=100
HOME=/home
INACTIVE=60
EXPIRE=
SHELL=/bin/bash
SKEL=/etc/skel
CREATE_MAIL_SPOOL=yes

previous_attempts.scan_output: Single-rule scan failed for account_disable_post_pw_expiration (rule=xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_account_disable_post_pw_expiration). Could not verify.

llm_verdict.message: The remediation has been successfully applied: the `/etc/default/useradd` file now contains `INACTIVE=60`, ensuring that accounts are disabled after password expiration, reducing the attack surface. No further actions are required.

previous_attempts.scan_output: Single-rule scan failed for account_disable_post_pw_expiration (rule=xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_account_disable_post_pw_expiration). Could not verify.

llm_verdict.message:
changing the number after INACTIVE= to 60.
        #   But note: we must preserve the rule name and the spacing.
        #
        #   We'll do: 
        #     If the line matches, we change the number after INACTIVE= to 60 and leave the rest.
        #   We can use sed in our mind? But we are not allowed to use the sed command. However, we can use the shell's string manipulation.
        #
        #   Since we are in a bash shell, we can use:
        #     new_line=${line/INACTIVE=[0-9]*/INACTIVE=60}
        #   But note: the above will replace every occurrence of INACTIVE= followed by a number? We don't want that.
        #
        #   Instead, we do:
        #     We'll match the pattern and then use a temporary variable to rebuild.
        #
        #   Let's do: 
        #     If the line matches the pattern, then we set:
        #         prefix=${line%% account_disable_post_pw_expiration*}
        #         suffix=${line#* account_disable_post_pw_expiration}
        #         But note: the pattern might be in the middle.
        #
        #   This is getting complex. Let's do a simple thing: we know the line has the string "account_disable_post_pw_expiration" and then a space and then "INACTIVE=" and then a number.
        #   We can split the line by the string "account_disable_post_pw_expiration" and then take the rest and split the rest by "INACTIVE=".
        #
        #   Actually, we can do:
        #     We'll change the line by replacing the substring "INACTIVE=30" with "INACTIVE=60".
        #
        #   We can do:
        #     new_line=${line/INACTIVE=30/INACTIVE=60}
        #
        #   But note: this will replace every occurrence of INACTIVE=30? We don't want that if there are multiple.
        #
        #   We are only concerned with the one that is in the rule.
        #
        #   Let's assume the line is exactly: "account_disable_post_pw_expiration INACTIVE=30"
        #   Then we can do:
        #     new_line="account_disable_post_pw_expiration INACTIVE=60"
        #
        #   But if there is extra text, we must be careful.
        #
        #   We decide: we will do a simple string replacement for the specific token.
        #
        #   We'll use: 
        #     new_line=${line/ INACTIVE=30 / INACTIVE=60}
        #
        #   However, if there are multiple occurrences of INACTIVE=30 in the line, we change them all? We don't want to change other numbers.
        #
        #   We are only to change the INACTIVE for the rule account_disable_post_pw_expiration.
        #
        #   Therefore, we do:
        #     We change only the first occurrence of the pattern "INACTIVE=30" that is after the rule? 
        #
        #   Given the complexity and the fact we are only allowed to use the three commands, we assume the line is simple and only has the rule and the value.
        #
        #   We'll do:
        if [[ "$line" == "account_disable_post_pw_expiration INACTIVE=30" ]]; then
            new_line="account_disable_post_pw_expiration INACTIVE=60"
        else
            new_line="$line"
        fi
        new_lines+=("$new_line")
    done

# Write the new content back to the file
printf '%s\n' "${new_lines[@]}" | write_file /etc/default/useradd

# Remediation script for Set Account Expiration Following Inactivity
# Change INACTIVE from 30 to 60 for the account_disable_post_pw_expiration rule in /etc/default/useradd

# Read the file
content=$(read_file /etc/default/useradd)

# Split content into lines
IFS=$'\n'
lines=($content)
unset IFS

# Prepare new lines array
new_lines=()

# Process each line
for line in "${lines[@]}"; do
    # Check if the line contains the account_disable_post_pw_expiration rule
    if [[ "$line" =~ account_disable_post_pw_expiration[[:space:]]*INACTIVE=[0-9]+ ]]; then

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly sets the account expiration to 60 days in /etc/default/useradd, which is a standard and effective practice to disable inactive accounts and reduce the attack surface.
